[[Jim Calder (rugby league)|Jim Calder]] (grandfather) }} }} '''Quentin Lee Pongia'''[http://nzleague.co.nz/kiwis/player_profile.php?letter=P&id=634 PONGIA, QUENTIN LEE 1992 - 2000 - KIWI #634] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120402170959/http://nzleague.co.nz/kiwis/player_profile.php?letter=P&id=634|date=2 April 2012}} ''nzleague.co.nz'' (9 July 1970 – 18 May 2019) was a New Zealand [[rugby league]] footballer who represented [[New Zealand national rugby league team|New Zealand]] in the 1990s and 2000s. ==Background== He was the grandson of fellow [[New Zealand national rugby league team|Kiwi international]] [[Jim Calder (rugby league)|Jim Calder]]. His sister Megan Tahapeehi has represented the [[Kiwi Ferns]],Coffey and Wood ''The Kiwis: 100 Years of International Rugby League'' {{ISBN|1-86971-090-8}} and his older brother [[Brendon Pongia|Brendon]] represented New Zealand as a professional basketball player.{{cite news|url=https://www.nzherald.co.nz/sport/news/article.cfm?c_id=4&objectid=12233454|access-date=7 June 2019|title=Kiwis' rugby league legend Quentin Pongia's last days – old videos, rain on the roof and laughter|date=25 May 2019|newspaper=The New Zealand Herald}} ==Playing career== Originally from the [[West Coast, New Zealand|West Coast]], Pongia transferred east in 1988. He registered and played for the Riccarton Knights Rugby League Football Club in the [[Canterbury Rugby League]] competition, representing the province in 1991 and 1992.''New Zealand Rugby League Annual '98'', [[New Zealand Rugby Football League]], 1998. p.4 Pongia later played for the [[Canberra Raiders]], and was part of the 1994 Grand Final winning premiership team. He also became a [[New Zealand national rugby league team|New Zealand]] international and was selected to go on the [[1993 New Zealand rugby league tour of Great Britain and France]], playing in all five [[Test match (rugby league)|test match]]es. He played at the [[1995 Rugby League World Cup]]. Pongia played for the [[Auckland Warriors]] and was the [[New Zealand Rugby League|NZRL]] player of the year in 1998. He joined the [[Sydney Roosters]] of the [[National Rugby League]] before heading to France. Pongia went to France as player coach of Paris Chattilon, but it didn't work out and he moved to [[Villeneuve Leopards|Villeneuve]], winning both [[Elite One Championship|the Championship]] and the [[Lord Derby Cup]] with the club. In 2003, after the French season had finished, Pongia returned to Australia and played two matches for the [[St. George Illawarra Dragons]]. He then finished his career in England playing for the [[Wigan Warriors]]. Pongia played for the [[Wigan Warriors]] at prop forward in the [[2003 Super League Grand Final]] which was lost to the [[Bradford Bulls]]. He was forced to retire from rugby league after it was discovered that he suffered from [[hepatitis B]].[http://www.wiganwarriors.com/SquadMember.asp?teamid=9&id=167 Quentin Pongia] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20071216093532/http://www.wiganwarriors.com/SquadMember.asp?teamid=9&id=167|date=16 December 2007}} ''Wigan Warriors'' ==Post playing== In 2009 Pongia was the strength and conditioning coach for the Canberra Raiders [[NRL Under-20s|Toyota Cup (Under-20s)]] team. He was promoted to assistant coach for the Raiders [[National Rugby League]] team for the 2010 season.[http://www.nrl.com/NewsViews/LatestNews/NewsArticle/tabid/10874/newsId/56889/Default.aspx McFadden, Pongia join Raiders staff] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120405070618/http://www.nrl.com/NewsViews/LatestNews/NewsArticle/tabid/10874/newsId/56889/Default.aspx |date=5 April 2012 }} ''AAP'', 25 September 2009 During the [[2009 Rugby League Four Nations|2009 Four Nations]] Pongia was an assistant trainer and mentor for the [[New Zealand national rugby league team]].New Zealand Rugby League, ''The 2009 Kiwis'' (Auckland, Sentra Printing 2009) p.19 He was replaced as a Raiders assistant coach for the 2012 season by [[Justin Morgan (rugby league)|Justin Morgan]].[http://www.rleague.com/content/article.php?id=41539 Raiders appoint Justin Morgan as Assistant Coach] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20111020131608/http://www.rleague.com/content/article.php?id=41539|date=20 October 2011}} ''rleague.com'', 19 October 2011 Quentin Pongia was the wellbeing officer for the [[Manly-Warringah Sea Eagles]] in 2017.{{Cite web |url=http://gossip-coffee.com/index.php/2017/05/15/sea-eagles-former-nrl-star-looking-players-wellbeing/ |title=Sea Eagles - Former NRL star looking after players wellbeing |access-date=16 May 2017 |archive-date=8 June 2017 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170608100536/http://gossip-coffee.com/index.php/2017/05/15/sea-eagles-former-nrl-star-looking-players-wellbeing/ |url-status=dead }} ==Death== Pongia died of bowel cancer on 18 May 2019 in Greymouth, at the age of 48.{{Cite news|url=http://deaths.press.co.nz/obituaries/the-press-nz/obituary.aspx?n=quentin-pongia-q&pid=192926849&fhid=12784|title=Quentin "Q" Pongia|work=The Press|date=20 May 2019|access-date=21 May 2019}}{{cite news|url=https://www.nzherald.co.nz/sport/news/article.cfm?c_id=4&objectid=12232165|access-date=18 May 2019|title=Rugby League:
